claims.claim_text: The module opens with a dated Fortune 500 financial-services incident in January 2023.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | opening line
claims.rationale: This is a datelined real-world-style anecdote with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: A compromised GitHub account led to 11 hours of production admin access, 2.3 million records exfiltrated, and $18 million in costs.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: That IAM user's access keys were stored in a shared `.env` file in a private GitHub repo. When an employee left the company and their personal GitHub account was compromised three months later, the attacker found the keys, assumed the production role, and had full admin access to production infrastructure for 11 hours before CloudTrail alerts triggered. The attacker exfiltrated 2.3 million customer records. The breach cost $18 million in regulatory fines, legal fees, and customer notification.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Shared long-lived credentials in repositories can be abused after an unrelated account compromise, leading to unauthorized production access, data exposure, and major incident-response costs.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed breach narrative with specific timeline, impact, and cost figures.

claims.claim_text: AWS cross-account access commonly uses STS AssumeRole, which returns temporary credentials for cross-account access.
claims.span_hint: section: Cross-Account Role Assumption (AWS) | intro paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS STS documentation directly says AssumeRole returns temporary credentials and is typically used for cross-account access.

claims.claim_text: Without aws:PrincipalOrgID, any AWS account that knows the ARN could potentially assume the role if the trust policy names one account root.
claims.span_hint: section: The Role Chain Pattern | Stop and think answer
claims.anchor_text: *Without the org condition, any AWS account that explicitly allows its users to assume your role could potentially do so if they know the ARN and the trust policy only specifies `arn:aws:iam::111111111111:root` without external IDs. Always restrict trust to your organization.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *Without the `aws:PrincipalOrgID` condition, the role relies only on the principals named in the trust policy; adding the org condition gives you an additional organization-level boundary around who can assume it.*
claims.rationale: The original explanation overstates what a trust policy that names a specific account root permits.

claims.claim_text: AWS IAM Identity Center is the recommended centralized approach for workforce access across multiple AWS accounts.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Identity Center (AWS SSO) | intro paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS Prescriptive Guidance explicitly calls IAM Identity Center the recommended approach for centrally managed access to multiple AWS accounts.

claims.claim_text: When you assign a permission set, IAM Identity Center creates and manages corresponding IAM roles in each target account.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up IAM Identity Center with Terraform | paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The IAM Identity Center documentation directly describes creation and ongoing management of these account-local roles.

claims.claim_text: Workload Identity Federation can exchange a workload's existing credentials for short-lived Google Cloud credentials, and the impersonated service account can be in a different project.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP Workload Identity Federation Across Projects | intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Google's federation guide explicitly covers short-lived credential exchange and service account impersonation where the service account is in a different project.

claims.claim_text: The Kubernetes federation subject includes the namespace and ServiceAccount name, so bindings can be scoped to a specific namespace and service account.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP Workload Identity Federation Across Projects | Pause and predict answer
claims.rationale: Google's Kubernetes federation docs define the subject format as namespace plus ServiceAccount name.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft Entra Workload ID on AKS uses OIDC federation with service account tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Entra ID and Workload Identity | intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS overview explicitly describes OIDC federation using service account tokens.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft recommends Microsoft Entra Workload ID instead of pod-managed identity for AKS, and pod-managed identity was deprecated.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Entra ID and Workload Identity | intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's pod-managed identity page says Workload ID replaces pod-managed identity and records the deprecation.

claims.claim_text: For Microsoft Entra federated credentials, the audience should be api://AzureADTokenExchange and it defines the expected aud claim.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Entra ID and Workload Identity | Pause and predict answer
claims.rationale: Microsoft's federated credential documentation directly explains this audience value and its meaning.

claims.claim_text: AWS ABAC policies can compare principal tags and resource tags during authorization.
claims.span_hint: section: AWS ABAC with Tags
claims.rationale: The AWS ABAC tutorial directly shows policies that match principal tags and resource tags.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud IAM Conditions use a subset of Common Expression Language (CEL).
claims.span_hint: section: GCP ABAC with IAM Conditions
claims.rationale: Google's IAM Conditions overview explicitly says conditions use a subset of CEL.

claims.claim_text: In S3 Object Lock compliance mode, protected objects cannot be overwritten or deleted by any user, including the root user, until retention expires.
claims.span_hint: section: Centralized Audit Logging for Identity Events | Stop and think answer
claims.rationale: The S3 Object Lock documentation directly states this compliance-mode behavior.

claims.claim_text: A CloudTrail organization trail is applied to member accounts, and logging starts automatically for new accounts added to the organization.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Configurations for Identity Auditing | item 1
claims.rationale: CloudTrail's organization-trail docs directly describe member-account copies and automatic onboarding for newly added accounts.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes audit logs can answer what happened, when, who initiated it, and on what resource.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Configurations for Identity Auditing | item 2
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes auditing docs enumerate those exact questions that audit records help answer.

claims.claim_text: Just-in-time access uses time-bound, approval-based activation for elevated privileges.
claims.span_hint: section: Just-In-Time (JIT) Access | intro paragraph
claims.rationale: Microsoft's PIM guidance directly describes time-based and approval-based role activation for JIT access.

claims.claim_text: AWS IAM evaluates 2.5 billion authorization requests per second with a sub-millisecond latency target.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **AWS IAM evaluates an average of 2.5 billion authorization requests per second** across all AWS accounts globally. Every API call, every S3 object access, every Lambda invocation goes through IAM evaluation. The system has a design target of sub-millisecond evaluation latency, which is why IAM policies are evaluated locally at the service endpoint rather than at a central authorization server.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **AWS IAM evaluates authorization for a massive volume of requests across AWS services.** IAM decisions are designed to be fast enough that authorization does not become a practical bottleneck for normal service operations.
claims.rationale: The exact throughput and latency figures are not backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud Workload Identity Federation supports workloads on AWS or Azure and other OIDC or SAML identity providers instead of service account keys.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | item 2
claims.rationale: Google's Workload Identity Federation overview explicitly lists AWS, Azure, and OIDC/SAML providers and positions federation as an alternative to service account keys.

claims.claim_text: The confused deputy item correctly attributes the concept's origin and says every cross-account role should use aws:SourceArn and aws:SourceAccount.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **The concept of "confused deputy" attacks** was first described by Norm Hardy in 1988. In cloud IAM, it applies when a service (the "deputy") is tricked into using its own permissions on behalf of an unauthorized caller. AWS mitigates this with the `aws:SourceArn` and `aws:SourceAccount` conditions on trust policies. Every cross-account role should include these conditions to prevent confused deputy attacks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **The "confused deputy" problem** applies in cloud IAM when a more-privileged service or third party is tricked into acting for an unauthorized caller. In AWS, mitigations depend on the scenario: service principals commonly use `aws:SourceArn` or `aws:SourceAccount`, while third-party cross-account role assumption often relies on `sts:ExternalId`.
claims.rationale: The original mixes a historical attribution with an overgeneralized and partly incorrect mitigation rule.

claims.claim_text: Azure Entra ID processes over 90 billion authentications per day as of 2025 and is the largest identity provider in the world by transaction volume.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? | item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Azure Entra ID processes over 90 billion authentications per day** as of 2025, making it the largest identity provider in the world by transaction volume. This includes not just Azure cloud access but Microsoft 365, third-party SaaS applications, and on-premises Active Directory hybrid scenarios. When you federate AKS workload identity through Entra ID, your Kubernetes pods are participating in this same massively distributed identity fabric.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Microsoft Entra ID operates at very large global scale.** When you federate AKS workload identity through Entra ID, your workloads rely on the same identity platform used across Microsoft's cloud and SaaS ecosystem.
claims.rationale: The exact daily-authentication figure and world-largest superlative are not safely supportable from an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: New IAM Identity Center permission sets default to 1-hour sessions and are backed by IAM roles configured with a 12-hour maximum session duration.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | row: No session duration limits on admin roles
claims.rationale: AWS documents both the 1-hour default for new permission sets and the 12-hour maximum configured on the generated IAM roles.

claims.claim_text: Without source conditions, any account that can construct the right principal ARN could assume your role, so you should always add aws:PrincipalOrgID or aws:SourceAccount.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes | row: Forgetting to add aws:SourceAccount to trust policies
claims.anchor_text: Without source conditions, any account that can construct the right principal ARN could assume your role. Always add `aws:PrincipalOrgID` or `aws:SourceAccount`.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use source conditions such as `aws:SourceArn` or `aws:SourceAccount` for AWS service principals where supported; for third-party cross-account access, use controls such as `sts:ExternalId`, and use `aws:PrincipalOrgID` when you want to constrain access to your organization.
claims.rationale: The original advice conflates different AWS trust-policy mechanisms and overstates what they do.

further_reading.title: Delegate access across AWS accounts using IAM roles
further_reading.why_relevant: Good primary reference for STS role assumption and temporary credentials in cross-account AWS setups.

further_reading.title: About Workload Identity Federation for GKE
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers how GKE workload identity works, principal identifiers, and keyless access to Google Cloud APIs.

further_reading.title: Use Microsoft Entra Workload ID with AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary overview of AKS workload identity, OIDC federation, and service-account-based authentication.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Auditing
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains what Kubernetes audit logs capture and how they support identity and access investigations.

notes: No shared citation pool exists for this section yet, so source_ids are empty. Per contract, I excluded quiz items, hands-on exercise content, diagrams, and code blocks, and only flagged table rows when they asserted concrete vendor behavior or exact figures.
